rationale.R1.2: The non-stationarity argument (§3 proxy) contains a notable logical gap: asserting that distribution shift is 'reflected in the router's input space' does not guarantee the router's learned threshold remains calibrated under that shift. Checking premise-to-conclusion validity is load-bearing regardless of intent.
rationale.R1.3: Specific quantitative claims (63% compute reduction, 4% CRPS penalty, threshold calibrated on 2014–2017 valid through SSP5-8.5 projections) require grounding in reported experiments/tables. Evidence-claim alignment is central to any substantive reading of this excerpt.
rationale.R1.4: 'To our knowledge, this is the first work to apply learned per-query routing to climate emulation' is a load-bearing novelty claim; assessing it requires comparison against mixture-of-experts, speculative decoding, and adaptive-compute literature applied to earth-system models.
